summary: Joel Spolsky emphasizes that the foundation of any business must be solving a clear, identifiable pain point for customers. He argues that entrepreneurs must be able to articulate what problem they solve, for whom, and how customers will pay for the solution. He illustrates his point with an anecdote about attending six startup presentations where none could clearly explain what pain they addressed. This is excerpted from his foreword to Bob Walsh's book on running a one-person software company.
key_points: Every business must start with a clear pain point it solves for a specific customer
Entrepreneurs need to articulate not just the problem but the payment mechanism — how customers will pay to solve their pain
Most startups fail at the most basic requirement: clearly explaining what problem they solve
money_quotes: Don't start a business if you can't explain what pain it solves, for whom, and why your product will eliminate this pain, and how the customer will pay to solve this pain.
I went to a presentation of six high tech startups and not one of them had a clear idea for what pain they were proposing to solve.
key_insight: A business that cannot clearly articulate the specific pain it solves for a specific customer has no foundation to build on.
